## Understanding Optics and Photonics

Optics and photonics both study light and its uses in practical applications. Precision is vital in these areas for
proper function. As systems get smaller, proper alignment maintains quality and power control. 

### Optics

Optics is a branch of physics that studies the behavior and properties of light and how instruments use or detect
it. Optical devices like lenses, mirrors, and prisms manipulate light and have various uses, from microscopes to
fiber optics. 

### Photonics

Photonics is a branch of optics science dealing with generating and manipulating light in the form of photons.
Various processes enable light particle manipulation, including amplification, signal processing, and
sensing.

# Versatile parallel signal processing with a scalable silicon photonic chip

[*Nature Communications*](/ncomms)
**volume 16**, Article number: 288 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

9545 Accesses

8 Citations

3 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

Silicon photonic signal processors promise a new generation of signal processing hardware with significant advancements in processing bandwidth, low power consumption, and minimal latency. Programmable silicon photonic signal processors, facilitated by tuning elements, can reduce hardware development cycles and costs. However, traditional programmable photonic signal processors based on optical switches face scalability and performance challenges due to control complexity and transmission losses. Here, we propose a scalable parallel signal processor on silicon for versatile applications by interleaving wavelength and temporal optical dimensions. Additionally, it incorporates ultra-low-loss waveguides and low-phase-error optical switch techniques, achieving an overall insertion loss of 10 dB. This design offers low loss, high scalability, and simplified control, enabling advanced functionalities such as accurate microwave reception, narrowband microwave photonic filtering, wide-bandwidth arbitrary waveform generation, and high-speed parallel optical computing without the need for tuning elements calibration. Our programmable parallel signal processor demonstrates advantages in both scale and performance, marking a significant advancement in large-scale, high-performance, multifunctional photonic systems.

To date, various silicon programmable photonic signal processors have been proposed. Intensive studies have focused on developing reconfigurable feedforward coherent multiport interferometers architectures using cascades of beam-splitters or Mach Zehnder Interferometers (MZIs) with phase-tuning elements[14](#ref-CR14 "Clements, W. More flexible architectures use the structure of two-dimensional feedback optical switch-based meshes. These meshes allow highly reconfigurable optical signal manipulation and enable functionalities such as tapped delayed line filters, beamforming, frequency measurements, and matrix calculation[9](#ref-CR9 "Pérez-López, D. et al. Efforts to reduce chip losses and extend delays have focused on optimizing mode field distribution and improving fabrication processes[33](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#ref-CR33 "Pafchek, R. et al. Ultralow‐Loss Silicon Photonics beyond the Singlemode Regime. Laser Photonics Rev. 16, 2100292 (2022)."). These waveguides have losses as low as 0.14 dB/cm. Based on this technique, a 4-channel chip-scale programmable photonic signal processor was realized, enabling functions such as tunable time-delay (TDL) and microwave photonic beamforming[8](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#ref-CR8 "Xie, Y. et al. Low-loss chip-scale programmable silicon photonic processor. OEA 6, 220030 (2023)."). Additionally, broadened silicon photonic waveguides have proven effective in enhancing fabrication tolerance and minimizing random phase errors during light propagation[36](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#ref-CR36 "Song, L., Li, H. & Dai, D. Mach–Zehnder silicon-photonic switch with low random phase errors. Opt. Lett. 46, 78 (2021)."). This allows to simplify the configuration of large-scale programmable photonic signal processors, compared with other methods such as improved processor architectures[37](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#ref-CR37 "Xu, P. & Zhou, Z. Silicon-based optoelectronics for general-purpose matrix computation: a review. Adv. Photon. 4, 044001-1-13 (2022).") or self-configuring algorithms[7](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#ref-CR7 "Zhou, H. et al. Self-Configuring and Reconfigurable Silicon Photonic Signal Processor. ACS Photonics 7, 792–799 (2020)."),[38](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#ref-CR38 "Catalá-Lahoz, C., Pérez-López, D., Huy-Ho, T. & Capmany, J. Self-configuring programmable silicon photonic filter for integrated microwave photonic processors. APL Photonics 8, 116103 (2023)."),[39](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#ref-CR39 "Pérez-López, D., López, A., DasMahapatra, P. & Capmany, J. Multipurpose self-configuration of programmable photonic circuits. Nat. Commun. 11, 6359 (2020).").

In this study, we propose a scalable programmable signal processor on silicon for versatile optical and microwave photonic signal processing by interleaving wavelength and temporal optical dimensions. Our design integrates two 16-channel wavelength-division-multiplexing (WDM)-based (de)multiplexers for wavelength selection and amplitude control, and 6-bit TDLs for temporal signal manipulation. The device enables parallel signal processing in each channel with an overall loss depending only on a single channel, ensuring scalability and effective management of complex tasks. We utilize ultra-low-loss waveguide spirals with a broadened cross-section of 3 × 0.22 μm². The phase shifters in the arms of the optical switches are widened to 2 µm to achieve low random phase errors due to fabrication imperfections. A pair of dual-elliptical rings (DERs) are used as WDM-based (de)multiplexers, carefully designed to maximize the free spectral range (FSR) for broad optical bandwidths[40](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#ref-CR40 "Xu, S., Wang, J., Yi, S. & Zou, W. High-order tensor flow processing using integrated photonic circuits. Nat. Commun. 13, 7970 (2022)."). Our silicon photonic processor occupies a chip area of about 14.05 mm², with each TDL channel offering a tuning range from 0 to 201.6 ps. The waveguide propagation losses is about 0.14 dB/cm, and the overall chip insertion loss is around 10 dB including the fiber-chip coupling losses. This parallel signal processor offers versatile high-performance functionalities, including accurate microwave reception, narrowband microwave photonic filtering, wide-bandwidth arbitrary waveform generation, and high-speed parallel optical computing. This work lays the groundwork for high-performance, large-scale programmable photonic processors, poised to revolutionize various optical applications and accelerate the development of integrated photonics for the next generation.

## Results

### Design and fabrication

The proposed versatile parallel signal processor is illustrated conceptually in Fig. [1a](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#Fig1). The design enables parallel signal processing through multi-channel, multi-wavelength, and multi-delay states coordination. Multi-wavelength lasers or microcomb source serve as the light source. Each wavelength is demultiplexed by an on-chip wavelength-division demultiplexer based on first DER array. The demultiplexed signals are send to the TDL channel, where each channel can be tuned individually to control the delay (optically) /phase (electrically) of the signals. At the output, delayed signals are routed through optical switches. One output port is combined and amplitude-controlled by the second tunable DER array, while another output port is directly guided to the edge coupler array for detection. On the basis of this approach, system-level demonstrations are presented for several major integrated photonics fields that require ultra-wide bandwidth, high-speed operation, and low power consumption, as shown in the insets of Fig. [1a](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#Fig1). These systems include: (a) Microwave beamforming reception is demonstrated with controllable steering angle and beamwidth by tuning the delay, amplitude and channel number. The beam achieves a 10.1° width with a wide ±75° angle range, facilitating accurate and wide-angle beam reception. (b) Narrow-band microwave photonic filters with controllable FSR (5–34.3 GHz)/3-dB bandwidth (3.4−11.9 GHz) is demonstrated. A continuous waveform envelope arbitrary waveform at radio frequency (RF) of 312.5 GHz is also demonstrated. (c) Parallel convolution-operation technology at 1.25 TOPs with configurable kernels is demonstrated. The maximum number of processing channels (kernel size) is 16.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-024-55162-5/MediaObjects/41467_2024_55162_Fig1_HTML.png)

**a** Conceptual illustration of the proposed silicon photonic signal processor for multi-disciplinary applications. **b** Schematic diagram of the proposed processor structure. **c** Optical microscopy image of the fabricated photonic chip. **d** Assembled chip with wire-bonded to control unit and accessed with fiber arrays.

In this work, a 16-channel parallel signal processor is proposed and demonstrated to implement this concept, and the chip layout is shown in Fig. [1b](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#Fig1) It comprises 1 × 16 DER-based demultiplexer, optical-switch-based 16 channels TDL, and a 16 × 1 DER-based multiplexers. The DERs are connected in cascade to form the wavelength (de)multiplexers and they are equipped with tuning elements to ensure wavelength alignment across channels for flat optical filtering and low-loss transmission. Each TDL channel comprises a 6-bit digitally-TDL with 7 thermo-optic switches based on MZIs, providing tunable time delays ranging from 0 to 201.6 ps (*T*total) with a 3.2 ps step (Δ*τ*). In order to conveniently test individual channels and reduce the overall device loss, we used grating couplers to test each channel separately (otherwise, more crossings would be needed to use edge couplers). This architecture minimizes the circuit footprint and heat dissipation, as detailed in Supporting Information, Section [S5](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#MOESM1). The chip was fabricated using a standard silicon MPW foundry with a 220 nm-thick silicon core. The optical microscopy image is depicted in Fig. [1c](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#Fig1). More details about the fabrication method can be found in Supporting Information, Section [S1](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#MOESM1). The overall chip footprint is 3.65 × 3.85 mm2. The fully-packaged chip with a thermo-electric cooler (TEC) is shown in Fig. [1d](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#Fig1), where all of the tuning units are wire-bonded to a carrier printed circuit board and optical I/Os are accessed with optical fiber arrays. All the tuning building blocks are controlled by a programmable control circuit (PCC) for reconfiguration.

### Chip characterization

The major challenges for realizing large-scale processor are reducing chip loss and programming complexity while maintaining a compact footprint. In this work, we introduced high-performance components to overcome this challenge. First, we utilized low-loss broadened silicon photonic waveguides with a compact Euler curve technique to reduce the waveguide loss and improve the fabrication tolerance, as shown in Fig. [2a](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#Fig2). More details about the loss reducing method, Euler curve design can be found in Supporting Information, Sections [S3.1](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#MOESM1) and [S3.2](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#MOESM1). The measured result shows that our low-loss waveguides have a uniform loss at 0.14 dB/cm across 10 chips, which is 15 time lower than that of singlemode waveguide (2 dB/cm). This improved fabrication tolerance also enhances delay accuracy, as detailed in Supporting Information, Section [S3.4](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#MOESM1).

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-024-55162-5/MediaObjects/41467_2024_55162_Fig2_HTML.png)

**a** Low-loss silicon waveguide. Optical microscopy image and the measured loss the proposed low-loss waveguides compared with the singlemode waveguide over 10 chips. **b** Calibration-free optical switch based on MZI. Optical microscopy image and measure transmittances spectrum without tuning. Inset shows the measured switching speed. **c** 16-channel delayline array. Optical microscopy image, measured delay result of the 6-bit TDL as the time delay varies from 3.2 ps (i.e., Δτ) to 201.6 ps (i.e., 63Δτ) and the corresponding delay variation for 6 states in 16 channels. **d** 16-channel DERs array. Optical microscopy image, simulation and experimental transmittances spectrum and wavelength equal and selective function with input 16-channels multiplexed wavelength (upper: input signal with uneven optical powers, middle: amplitude equalization, lower: wavelength selection with channel frequency gap of 400 GHz.).

Secondly, we introduce calibration-free MZI optical switches with broadened phase-shifters, so that the random phase imbalance is significantly reduced[41](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#ref-CR41 "Song, L. et al. Toward calibration-free Mach–Zehnder switches for next-generation silicon photonics. Photon. Res. 10, 793 (2022)."),[42](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#ref-CR42 "Song, L. et al. Low-Loss Calibration-Free 2 × 2 Mach-Zehnder Switches With Varied-Width Multimode-Interference Couplers. J. Lightwave Technol. 40, 5254–5259 (2022)."). In this way, one realizes calibration-free MZI optical switches, enabling near-perfect cross-state routing initially even without any additional power. Thus, it is possible to apply the same power (i.e., the bias voltage) for all optical switches to switch the bar-state. The fabricated MZI optical switch (Fig. [2b](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#Fig2)) exhibits a low insertion loss of 0.38 dB, an extinction ratio over 25 dB within 60 nm, a power consumption of 25 mW/π and a switching speed of 14 μs. A higher ER is always desirable for improving the system performance Due to the calibration-free nature of the present MZI optical switches, individual calibration for these MZI optical switches one by one is no longer demanded. These calibration-free optical switches notably reduce calibration complexity and power consumption, facilitating large-scale silicon photonic processors. The design details and the power consumption in the system can be found in Supporting Information, Section [S4](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#MOESM1) and [S2.3](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#MOESM1). The combination of low-loss waveguide spirals and optical switches forms the TDL. The 16-channel TDL and the representative result of the time delay are shown in Fig. [2c](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#Fig2). The measured time delay for channel 1 shows a high delay accuracy with delay time switching from 3.2 ps (i.e., Δ*τ*) to 201.6 ps (i.e., (26−1)Δ*τ*). Owing to the large fabrication tolerance of the broadened waveguides, the delay variation for different states across all channels remained below ±1 ps. This feature offers promising applications in various fields, including beamforming systems[26](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#ref-CR26 "Moreira, R. L. et al. Integrated Ultra-Low-Loss 4-Bit Tunable Delay for Broadband Phased Array Antenna Applications. IEEE Photon. Technol. Lett. 25, 1165–1168 (2013)."), optical computing[27](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#ref-CR27 "Xu, X. et al. 11 TOPS photonic convolutional accelerator for optical neural networks. Nature 589, 44–51 (2021)."), and optical buffers[28](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#ref-CR28 "Liu, J., Lee, T. T., Jiang, X. & Horiguchi, S. Blocking and Delay Analysis of Single Wavelength Optical Buffer With General Packet Size Distribution. J. Lightwave Technol. 27, 955–966 (2009)."). The simulation results of large fabrication tolerance on different applications are shown in Supporting Information, Section [S3.5](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#MOESM1).

![figure 3](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-024-55162-5/MediaObjects/41467_2024_55162_Fig3_HTML.png)

**a** Working principle of the microwave photonic beamforming receiving ends function using the proposed processor. **b** Measured phase response and **c** group delay response of the microwave signals for 6 delay states over 16 channels. **d** Calculated beam patterns corresponding to different number of channels at different steering angles with RF frequency *f* = 20 GHz. **e** Beam patterns at different RF frequencies (7–27 GHz) at the steering angle of 22.56°. **f** The operated voltage of the tuning devices for achieving given beam pattern (R: DERs, S: optical switch).

In addition, the DERs with adiabatically varied radii/widths (Fig. [2d](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#Fig2)) are designed to shrink cavity length and reduce loss. This design enables DERs to implement large bandwidth, flat-top filtering, and large FSR for 16-channel wavelength (de)multiplexing. The details and layout of the design DERs parameters can be found in the Supporting Information, Section [S6](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#MOESM1). The simulated and experimental transmittance spectra are shown in Fig. [2d](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#Fig2). It shows the resonance wavelengths have a channel spacing of 200 GHz and a 3-dB bandwidth of 0.6 nm, with a low-loss of around 1 dB. The slight transmission difference between each DER and the broadened passbands is due to the fabrication errors of the gap between two elliptical rings. Each DER is attached with a thermal-tuning micro-heater, allowing wavelength selection and amplitude equalization of input signals, essential for systems using multiple wavelengths. In Fig. [2d](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#Fig2), we also demonstrated the wavelength equalization and selection function with 16-channels multiplexed wavelength input. The channel spacing of the input signal is 200 G (source detail can be found in Methods). Evidently, the processor equalized their amplitudes and adjusted the laser frequency spacing from 200 GHz to 400 GHz. The total insertion loss of the packaged chip is around 10 dB, including the edge-coupling (1.8 dB/facet) and on-chip losses. The proposed architecture with excellent scalability and flexible tunability is significant for the development of WDM systems for photonic signal processing, microwave photonics, and optical computing. The detail information of the device scalability is shown in Supporting Information, Section [3.3](/articles/s41467-024-55162-5#MOESM1).

enriched_points.expanded_content: Developing advanced engineering solutions, such as high-precision motion control stages, enhances the alignment and performance of photonic systems in diverse applications.
Investigating novel materials, like ultra-low-loss silicon waveguides, is crucial for improving efficiency and scalability in photonics-based signal processing technologies.
Combining photonics with quantum technologies enables breakthroughs in processing speed, security, and capacity, paving the way for next-generation communication systems.
